era_context: Early Iron Age II in the Levant and Eastern Mediterranean. The kingdoms of Israel and Judah are established as separate entities. Egypt's 22nd Dynasty (Libyan) continues under Osorkon I. Phoenician city-states are at the height of their maritime power. Assyria remains relatively weak. Greece is in the Geometric period. This is a period of relative stability in the Levant, with regional powers pursuing independent policies.

events.title: Rehoboam of Judah continues reign; possible aftermath of Egyptian campaign
events.description: Rehoboam's reign continues in Judah. If Shoshenq I's campaign occurred in 925 BCE (or nearby years), 927 BCE would fall in the period following the Egyptian invasion, during which Judah would be recovering from military losses and tribute payments. The kingdom's economy and military capacity would have been strained. Archaeological evidence from Judahite sites shows continued settlement and modest fortification activity during this period, consistent with a kingdom rebuilding after external pressure.

sources.name: Archaeological survey of Judahite Iron Age II sites

events.certainty_note: Rehoboam's reign is traditionally dated c. 930–913 BCE. The specific events of 927 BCE are not independently documented; this entry describes the general context of his reign during this period.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of Judahite political consolidation (-930 onwards)

events.title: Jeroboam I's reign continues; northern Kingdom of Israel stable
events.description: Jeroboam I continues to rule the northern Kingdom of Israel. Archaeological evidence from northern Levantine sites shows continued settlement and economic activity. The kingdom maintains control of important trade routes and agricultural lands in the Jezreel Valley and surrounding regions. No major military or political crises are recorded for this year.

sources.name: Archaeological survey of Iron Age II northern Levantine sites

events.certainty_note: Jeroboam's reign is traditionally dated c. 930–909 BCE. No specific events are recorded for 927 BCE; this entry describes the general context of his reign.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are recorded for 927 BCE in surviving sources. The entries above describe the general context of the reigns of Rehoboam and Jeroboam I, but no contemporary sources document events specific to this year. The dating of major events in this period (e.g., Shoshenq I's campaign, the end of Rehoboam's reign) remains disputed, with scholarly estimates differing by 10–20 years.
historiographic_note: For 927 BCE, the historical record is essentially silent. No contemporary sources document specific events for this year. The entries above are inferences based on the known reigns of Rehoboam and Jeroboam I and the general archaeological context of Iron Age II in the Levant. The absence of documented events for this year is typical for ancient history; most years leave no written record. The chronological framework itself (placing events in specific years) is a modern scholarly construction based on biblical genealogies, Egyptian king lists, and astronomical calculations, all of which have significant uncertainties.

graph_edges.note: Rehoboam's reign continues toward its end c. 913 BCE
Jeroboam I's reign continues toward its end c. 909 BCE
